target_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: [Verified 2026-03-17 | Anal Biochem 1987] Halsted established the first formal surgical residency training program in the United States, cementing the institutional root of modern surgical education.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Epidemiol Infect 2016] Finney served as Halsted's first resident and primary deputy, serving as the critical bridge to the next generation of American general surgeons.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Bull Hist Med 2018] Bloodgood trained under Halsted and institutionalized surgical pathology as a mandatory core competency within surgical training.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Anal Biochem 1987] Young trained as an assistant surgeon under Halsted before being appointed to head the genitourinary dispensary in 1897.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Catheter Cardiovasc Interv 2017] Cushing served as Halsted's resident and later established the Hunterian Laboratory.

[Verified 2026-03-17 | J Orthop Sports Phys Ther 2000] Homans completed early surgical training under Halsted before joining Cushing at Brigham, heavily influenced by Halsted's physiological approach.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Anal Biochem 1987] Heuer served as Halsted's chief resident and later became chairman of surgery at Cornell/New York Hospital.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Br Med J 1953] Brooks trained at Hopkins under Halsted before moving to Washington University and eventually Vanderbilt.

evidence_locator: UCSF Department of Surgery Historical Archives; Naffziger Society History
notes: Naffziger served as assistant resident surgeon at Johns Hopkins under Halsted and Cushing, recognizing the superiority of the Halstedian training system before returning to build UCSF.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Anal Biochem 1987] Reid served as Halsted's resident and was a key adopter of his meticulous tissue handling techniques.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Warfield M. Firor received his A&S degree from Johns Hopkins in 1917 and his MD in 1921, training in surgery under the Halstedian system. Halsted died in September 1922, making Firor among the last surgeons formed directly within the original Halsted program. Firor became professor of surgery at the School of Medicine and acting surgeon-in-chief of the hospital from 1939 to 1941. He was best known for pioneering work on tetanus toxin and its effect on the spinal cord. He chaired the American Board of Surgery and the Joint Conference Committee on Graduate Training in Surgery. JHU awarded him an honorary doctorate in 1980. He died in 1988 at age 91. An endowed professorship in his name is currently held at Hopkins.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Int Rev Neurobiol 1968] Holman was Halsted's final chief resident before Halsted's death in 1922.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Hart trained at Hopkins under Halsted's system and was recruited to Duke as the founding chairman of surgery.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: [Verified 2026-03-17 | Can J Surg 2020] Blalock trained directly under the Halstedian system at Hopkins before returning as Chief of Surgery to perform the first blue baby operation.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Blakemore trained at Hopkins before establishing the Columbia vascular lineage, linking early synthetic graft innovation back to Halsted.
